sentences.text: The food, he brought us mostly in the places where we were before we moved during the first four months because, already, already, food was scarce.
That's right.
After moving to the ghetto, I haven't seen him--
--anymore.
He was not allowed to come in after May 1 anyway because it was sealed, but I didn't remember seeing him there at all.
I may have seen him when we moved in, and he may have been around, but that was about it.
So we lost complete contact with him.
No.
First of all, it was gradual anyway.
We moved certain-- a bed.
Once we got the room, we moved the bed in.
And whoever family was living there-- it was some, some laborer with his family-- and he moved out, and as soon as it became available, we moved in.
You had to move in right away, otherwise, you know, we won't get it.
So you had to occupy it because it's all a squatters, a kind of a process.
So we moved in probably in several days, you know, moved in a certain thing.
Actually, I rented a little wagon, you know, and we pushed it.
And I remember, just to give you an idea how people react to things.
We were walking.
I remember my mother and I and my father were pushing the wagon, and I think we had the bed on it.
And I don't know where he got the wagon.
Maybe my father in connection with his laborers that he had in the lumber yard, maybe he got something.
I don't recall how he wound up with that wagon on two wheels, and we just dragged it.
And it was snowy.
It was a very cold winter, and there was an old, bearded Jew walking next to us in his wagon, dragging something.
And he turned to my father.
And since Jews particularly are inclined to contemplation and philosophizing, he says to my father, look, he says, my life is obviously worth nothing.
Why am I doing this?
And my father says, look at this philosopher.
He's got himself time now to philosophize.
So, you know, this is the moods, and this is the types of situations that you are involved.
You are doing both.
You are attaining sanity and doing insane things.
Yes.
I would say six weeks to two months.
Yes.
And because my street was already-- probably where I came from probably was out of bounds for Jews by then.
Well, schools immediately started.
They, again, moved around, one building then another.
I think three buildings eventually wound up in the outskirts.
We had-- part of the ghetto had a little bit of free land, open land, because there was-- they included the Jewish cemetery.
And the Jewish cemetery was outside after some free space of fields.
And so that's where the school eventually wound up, which was nice because there were some trees, you know, a nice environment, but first we were in a building in the center of the ghetto.
So school immediately continued.
I mean, they tried to keep the school going all the time with interruptions, of course, whether it's a week or three weeks or whatever.
I don't recall dates.
And then we continued the school, and when the ghetto closed, we already were in the school.
And I was going to school, so the daily life was built around it.
Now as far as employment is concerned, needless to say, the ghetto-- well, not needless to say, let's say-- the ghetto evolved too.
It wasn't in one decision what it will be.
Reading about it today, I understand that there was a lot of question whether this ghetto will exist.
Whether it will be liquidated now or a month or a year or something later, so it was all evolving.
And now I'm not going to sit here and tell you the history of ghetto life because there are books written on it and chronicle.
So I would leave it to those who want to read about that.
All I say, that it did evolve and that employment, obviously there was none at the beginning.
And then they started opening places for work for the German industry, whatever they do, mostly, first of all, the tailor shops and then the leather shops and then even lumberyards.
I'm bringing lumberyards up because we had a big problem with my father going and finding a job.

My father basically, while maybe being-- remarking about the philosopher next to him asking this philosophical question, why am I pushing it if my life is worth nothing, my father actually adopted the same philosophy.
He felt that his life was not worth anything.
He was nothing to do with this.
He didn't see at this age why he has to adapt to that thing.
He was in the 50s.
I don't remember exactly.
But he was in the 50s.
He was much older than my mother, much older, about 16 or 17.
My mother immediately started looking for her work.
And she had got two offers as a matter of fact-- one to be a cleaning lady in some place and the other one to be an assistant to a manager of a kitchen.
And she was afraid to take the latter one because of the responsibility.
She was always a housewife.
She didn't know.
And I was furious.
I was 14 years old-- 15-- and I says, look.
I already understood what everybody today understood, and that's power.
You have to be in some position where you are better off than somebody else.
I instinctively learned.
I wasn't philosophizing at all.
I was just-- And I remember that we spent days agonizing about it.
And I was harassing my mother, and I was really-- that's just-- she says, well, I don't want to do things that I don't know that I'll fit.
I says, look, this is war.
You just-- you do it.
You just-- you will do fine.
And certainly she took the other job, thanks, probably, to my prodding.
No.
Of course.
Of course.
First of all, I understood exactly where the bread is buttered anyway.
Now we couldn't get any food out of that.
She couldn't steal any food.
I mean, people did steal food.
She did not because I don't know whether-- I don't think that we would want to do that.
And secondly, I think that maybe we were scared.
I don't know.
Look, when you are hungry, you will still anything.
So I'm not trying to suggest that we were sitting there and pontificating here that we were so honest that we could-- although I think that I have had some periods of time where I was going hungry.
I was always going hungry.
But I mean, my father was dying, and my mother didn't do anything to do anything improper.
But, but, but she ate there enough.
That means the piece of bread stayed home, and the soup stayed home.
And that was just about doubling it.
And that was a lot over a period.
One piece of bread of two kilograms for eight days, basically a large loaf of bread.
Again, don't pin me to the dates because that was evolving, but that's what I remember by most of the time.
And as far as soup is concerned, they would distribute vegetables to make your own soup.
Or if you worked, they would give you a soup, and usually it would be water with some one half potato in it, nothing profound.
It wasn't chunky soup, if that's what you're asking.
That's what you will get.
So vegetables would be distributed, usually potatoes or kohlrabi or whatever that was available.
And problems were always there, yes food, no food, and in the meantime, deportations, continuous deportations, you know, of young or old and so that-- at the same time.
Yeah, classmates.
Classmates were deported.
During the school year, not too many.
During the schooling, I mean.
School closed in October '41.
We have to jump ahead.
Until October '41, some, not too many.
I suspect that most people who are deported were people who had no connections at all with anybody, people who were brought in from small cities.
They would liquidate the small cities, the neighbor cities, and bringing people into the ghetto.
These people fell, you know, the first ones.
Selections to deportation were done by the local government, by the Jewish government itself-- you know, self-governing.
Everything was self governed.

The Judenrat, certainly I would not want to get involved at this point of discussing what the role of Chaim Rumkowski was, which was the Jewish King of Lodz Ghetto which many books and movies were made about.
And I think it's controversial to this very day what he did and how he did it.
But anyway, a lot of people were falling prey to it because they didn't have a connection.
They couldn't get a job.
It was somebody who made and placed them, and they were strangers in town.
A tragic, for example, was the story of the Prague and Viennese and the other Jews that were brought from Germany and herded into the Jewish-- into the Lodz Ghetto.
And these people were completely out of touch with the rest of the population, and they fell prey to both hunger, deprivation, and eventually deportations.
Nothing was obvious.
Nothing was simple.
There was-- any statement I am making is not inclusive, it's just statistically indicative of the times and of what was happening.
Well, my life was continued until '41 when the schooling.
My father refused to work, and then he finally went to-- see, he's got-- he knew all these guys in the lumber business.
So all he had to go and to talk to some of these guys who were running the lumberyard factory or whatever it was for furniture that was done that was going on in the ghetto.
But he didn't-- he was very hard, had a lot of quarrels with him on that subject.
I was a young.
Today I regret it very much.
Today I think he was right.

Unfortunately, when you are 15, 16 years-- 15 or 16 years old, you don't have that-- you cannot sympathize with a 50-plus old man who had his life already and felt that time has come to say goodbye and that this is not for him.
I did not see it that way.
I saw only one thing.
Either you survive or you die.
There are not other alternatives.
To him, there was an alternative and that's die.
But for a 15-year-old, dying is not an-- is not an option.
So we had a lot of hard words and times.
My father was working.
My mother participated in some of these arguments.
But they were painful.
For about a period of three, four months, my father actually closed the shutters-- there were shutters-- and never let-- wanted to see the light and stayed there.
Didn't shave, didn't do anything.
Then he went for work for a while, and then he did the same thing.
I think he was ready to die, and today I understand it very well.
I respect it, even.
At that time I was furious.
So that's one of the things that I regret from my personal life that I was caught into the process into the art of survival at all costs.
And my father didn't see it that way.
It's so painful sometimes to think about it because I remember the details, some of them, about the arguments, and they were not very pretty.
Did any of you get sick?
What was health care like?
There was no health care at all in the sense.
There were physicians.
You could go in, and you could pay probably some nominal amount of money.
There were local currency.
The ghetto has its own currency.
The kind of the ghetto issued his own currency with is own image in it and all the other things that were going with it.
So yes, you could go and see a physician.
There was a lady physician next to us, and I used to go and see her.

My problem has been to this very day-- and my father's-- I inherited it from my family, from my father's side-- and that's gastrointestinal problems, which are probably connected with emotion, as usually they are.
No.
I never had any problem.
Well, yeah, I would say so.
I had some problems, not too severe.
I had jaundice, for example.
To this very day I cannot give blood because I have jaundice.
Although I gave blood in the war in the independence in Israel.
I did give blood.
Nobody asked me the question.
But today when I would like to donate blood, and they ask me when I had jaundice.
I had it three times in a row.
My father probably died eventually of cancer of the colon.
And now I happen to work in the field of endoscopy, so I have my colon examined every three months by a colleague of mine.
So it's-- so it is-- so anyway, he died of that.
But I think that he died of willing-- I think he slowly died, and eventually there was-- yes, there was a physician.
I remember the last time I saw the lady physician, she came out and told my mother, forget it.
That's the end of it.
And I overheard it, and my mother didn't want me to hear it.
So I realized that my father was dying, and he died on the third of January 1943.
Before then I just want to mention one event, and that is in September 1942, which was a very critical time for the ghetto because that's when they took all the children.
And that include-- well, you know, it's very difficult to handle this subject.
I prefer to teach electrical engineering.
I'm not as emotionally involved in it.
That was my favorite cousin, Michael.
His mother, father, and brother died of hunger in the ghetto.
Plain, just you found them dead one at a time.
Michael was my favorite my-- I hope you don't mind this thing.
You know, it's difficult to get involved in that subject.
I really tried to push that subject back, but I realized the significance of it in my life.
And I think if I do a job, I have to do it all the way.
My son's name is Michael.
He is 30 years old today.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Michael was the youngest.
He was my favorite cousin.
He was two years younger than I am.
By the time he was sick or already had probably bone, bone cancer or whatever it was, or bone tuberculosis, they called it.
And my mother would find some crumbs of bread that she would bring, and his brother was still-- they were both insane with hunger.
My mother would walk in, and Michael would say, and give it first to Oleg because he is hungrier.
How can you be hungrier when you are both insane with hunger?
Anyway, the other died of hunger.
He was a big boy.
He needed a lot of food.
My mother saw that she cannot leave him alone, so she took him to the hospital.
And, of course, three days later they came after the hospitals.
And my mother was trying to snatch him out, ran around.
But the trucks were already there, and they just shoved them into the trucks like cattle, and I guess that's when he died.
So September 1942 was the critical time for the life of ghetto because we-- all the children disappeared.
Now a controversy about the King of Jews or this guy, Chaim Rumkowski, whatever can be said about him, I don't know.
And I, I presume he did help to survive.
After all, ghetto lasted the longest in the Nazi Germany, and survivors from Lodz are probably the largest percentage of survivors that went through.
So I would say that maybe he deserves some credit for it.
He claims that that's what he was doing.
On the other hand, he was the one who came out and asked the mothers to give the children, and he was a head of an orphanage before the war.
And you ask yourself the question, you know, what happens to a person like that when you read the memoirs of Adam Czerniakow, who was electrical engineer-- not that I'm biased to him.
In Warsaw, he was the head of the Judenrat.
And when he ask him for the children, he swallowed the cyanide pill.
And after September-- after September '42, things have changed dramatically.
There were not children anymore.
There were no old ones anymore.

My uncle, the one who never believed that, that people who created Goethe and Beethoven could possibly be that bad, that's the one they picked off the street in September 1942 because he had grey hair.
So by the time that period was over, my father died three months later in December in January 19-- 3rd January 1943.
And then my aunt, the one who you see the picture on that we used to go to the summer-- that we used to spend summers with, she got typhoid.
And after you recover from it, you have to eat a lot.
So her daughter, my favorite cousin, again, Lillian-- there is a picture of her there-- she decided to feed her mother her portion of bread without knowing-- letting her know.
Well, needless to say, after being completely without food for about two months, she developed, what is called, accelerated tuberculosis and burned up in about six weeks.
They didn't let me see her when she was sick.
And so that took care of just about everybody.
The only people that disappeared from our lives as far as my extended family was concerned was the parents of my cousin in Warsaw now.
They moved to other city, Lvov, because he was, at that time, directing theatre there, and they disappeared, of course, perished there.
The others were-- I related how they disappeared, all of them, by May, 1943.
And the only remaining extended family was my aunt with her older daughter.
And she was herded into on a truck in August, 1944 during the liquidation and probably sent to Auschwitz, directly into the gas chamber.
So the only thing that were left was my mother and I.
That was-- no, my aunt and her daughter stayed in Lodz Ghetto until the end in August, 1944.
No.
No, we lived in separate places.
They had another room somewhere.
Yeah, right.
So now the first work I did was a friend of one of my classmates, the father had a-- was directing one of the plants, which happened to be later.
So the whole class when there.
You know, I don't know, maybe 10, 15 of us went to that place.
And I worked there for several months.
I remember I worked there in '43 still because my father-- when my father died, I was still in that plant.
I remember that.
Then I started working.
About two months later, I went to work for a statistical office, which was running statistics.
Everything is very well organized in Germany and in the ghetto that's run by-- so we had some statistical office, and I became a runner sort of, one of those guys who delivers things.
And then that was closed.
And I was-- actually, Runkowski came in to liquidate it, and he was told by my boss that I finished high school-- finished high school age of 16.
We finished high school at 16, then I had two more years.
So he turned to me with a smile and said, hey, you got my diploma.
So I'll send you to a good place.
He sent me to a place which was a company that made-- factory that made telephones.
And I was inclined always to engineering and to things like that, so I enjoyed it very much.
I was treated very well and very nice people there, mostly more educated kids, either from my school.
A bunch of them came from Prague.
I remember I became very friendly with a whole bunch of them.
And some of them were communists, actually, and active in sabotage and in all kinds of things.
I don't know if you want me to describe some of the events of that time, just to give a feeling of that.
From there on I went to Auschwitz.
And we had, for example-- it's a very strange group of people.
The guy in charge was a, an engineer, [NON-ENGLISH].
His wife was actually a Gentile, and she came with him because she loved him, I guess.
And she was a very lovely lady as far as I was concerned.
And I actually went to see her after the war to tell her about her husband.
And, and the assistant director was a German from Latvia who looked like a Nazi and was a Nazi and, in fact, was in the Hitler Union, and a wonderful guy.
They wanted to be a pilot, I think.
I don't know the story.
I mean, you have to ask him.
He's somewhere.
He lives in New York.
I visited him.
And I don't know what he wanted to be.
You needed the pure Aryan something-or-other, and they found a grandmother or something.
They stuck him right into the ghetto.
He was one of the finest men that I have ever met.
Felix Brickman.
He's a wonderful guy.
He married a lady that worked in the plant, Sima, and he came back and they have a son.
I visited them in Long Island.
He has a plant in New York, I think.
I think he's got the textile, so it's really very nice.
I liked him very much.
He was always very nice to everybody.
He was a very nice man.
Anyway, she was actually-- I'll go back to her after the war because I met her before I saw him.
And then-- where was I?
You were talking about how they sabotaged things and--
Oh, yeah, then I was with the young kids.
And one of the things that we had is people were dying.
Of course, young people were getting this tuberculosis mostly, so they were dying like flies.
And so we did-- we pulled out of the concrete from the back of the plant, and we started growing vegetables.
And at that time, my friends-- you know, and I was somehow put in charge of growing.
Like we used to guard it and give the vegetables to the ones who really needed it.
But who was needing it?
It was really ridiculous because her mother would come in and say that her daughter is dying of tuberculosis.
We'll give her a carrot.
Still going?
And so anyway, the wife of the director wanted to be a co-chairman.
She needed something to do.
So I said, fine.
As a matter, matter, he promised me some milk or some other things, and I said fine.
Why not?
I was accused by my colleagues that I was cooperating with the capitalists.
I thought that that was most bizarre, and I think this left me with an infallible dislike and complete disdain for any party hacks of any sort because what the hell are they talking about?
What are you going to read Bukharin?
Are you going to read Marx?
And you're going to tell me.
Well, what kind of capitalist is this guy?
He died in front of me in Auschwitz, this poor slob.
What kind of a capitalist is he?
Oh, so he had a little bit better food for the time being, who cares?
But in the meantime, I can get something for somebody.
Why should I suddenly go into a class war?
There's enough war already.
And these kinds of things, I think, influenced me a lot in my behavior later on in terms of joining things, of being part of a group.
I was never a groupie, as I pointed out, even at 10 year old, but that certainly clinched it.
And there are fine people, you know, memoirs, diary by Dawid Sierakowiak who is printed all over the place, was my classmate.
He was a communist.
And, you know, I respect him very much.
He was very highly educated, but I couldn't see the whole-- the whole thing.
It was bordering on bizarre if it wasn't already.
And as a matter of fact, after the war-- and I'll go into Auschwitz shortly, so I'll discuss that-- and I have gone to see her because her husband obviously died.
And I tried to help him at the time in Auschwitz for reasons that I had an opportunity.
So anyway, that's, that's the spirit.
That's the kind of an example of what kind of a life was all about, the players and the-- and the scenery.
In that room at the very last moment.
Yes, ma'am.
Well, it was pretty cold in the winter time, obviously.
First of all, we started taking apart everything including the outhouse.
So wood disappeared.
Everything, all the fences.
Everything was taken.
Trees were cut.
Everybody was stealing wood to burn.
Eventually, there was some coal always distributed.
There was rationing of coal.
But we tried to do it.
One of the things that I survived maybe is that my mother, I was able to get from another kitchen some potato peels, which, at that time, was worth a fortune.
But we didn't have any water, so my mother would come at night and wash it, and I would bring it from the-- from the outside.
We used to have one of those--
No, water--
I guess I'm not control-- I'm not in command of this language yet?
No, I think it has to do with my memory, not with my vocabulary.
A water well that you pump, and so I would go out to the bucket, and I would get it.
See, the reason why I don't remember water well is because the world well doesn't apply to this subject.
Anyway, so I brought this water and we washed it.
And then since I was an electrical engineer, we had the meter outside, which was not metering the electricity but limiting it to 25 watts.
If you do more, you couldn't use it.
So we had one bulb for 25 watts.
That's all you can use.
So what I did is I had to made a short across it.
And I would then put the electricity, as much as I wanted it, and hide it inside.

Underneath the floor we had a little hole there which you would put food, cool, to keep it cool, I would put there a bucket of water, and I would boil the water, and my mother would mix it with the burned wheat that they gave us instead of coffee.
They gave us burnt wheat, which you couldn't eat.
It was bitter.
So we'd mixed it up, and I would bake it all night.
And the wire would be such that if somebody knocked on the door, my mother could pull it.
Or I could pull it, and if somebody walked in to check, you could not find it.
If they found it, that would have meant, of course, immediate deportation or, equivalently, death.
But anyway, so that was one of the things that I ventured to do in terms of I was an electrical engin-- that was my first life-saving electrical engineering thing.
Maybe that's why I worked in the defense so much here.
I don't know.
I guess this was not in place, but anyway.
